content:
Character: Marta Kovacs
Perception: Marta is not someone you'd notice in a crowd, and I think she's built her entire life around that fact — the black uniform, the hunched posture, the way she angles her laptop screen away from passersby. But her work is extraordinary: precise, textured, full of a melancholy beauty that feels Eastern European in the best sense. I sat down and told her so, and she looked at me like I'd pulled a knife. I commissioned a bookplate partly because I wanted one and partly because I wanted to see her face when someone valued what she made. She's guarded, prickly, self-deprecating. I suspect she hates how I look, not out of malice but because my appearance makes her own feel like a failure. That's not something I can fix, but I can keep paying her well and treating her work with the seriousness it deserves. I know she's Hungarian, freelance, talented, and profoundly lonely.
summary:
Character: Marta Kovacs
Perception: Marta is not someone you'd notice in a crowd, and I think she's built her entire life around that fact — the black uniform, the hunched posture, the way she angles her laptop screen away from passersby. But her work is extraordinary: precise, textured, full of a melancholy beauty that feels Eastern European in the best sense. I sat down and told her so, and she looked at me like I'd pulled a knife. I commissioned a bookplate partly because I wanted one and partly because I wanted to see her face when someone valued what she made. She's guarded, prickly, self-deprecating. I suspect she hates how I look, not out of malice but because my appearance makes her own feel like a failure. That's not something I can fix, but I can keep paying her well and treating her work with the seriousness it deserves. I know she's Hungarian, freelance, talented, and profoundly lonely.
